# Bottom trawl survey diversity index {#survey_shannon}

```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = F)
library(ecodata)
library(downloadthis)
```

*Last updated February 11, 2025*

**Description**: Diversity metric for the bottom trawl survey data

**Contributor(s)**: Andy Beet, Sean Lucey

**Affiliations**: NEFSC

**Indicator Family**: 

- [X] Megafauna


**Indicator Category**:

- [X] Published Methods
- [X] Database pull


**Synthesis Theme**:

- [X] Regime Shifts
- [X] Ecosystem Reorganization


```{r download_survey_shannon}
# Download dataset from ecodata
try(downloadthis::download_this(
ecodata::survey_shannon,
output_name = 'survey_shannon',
output_extension = '.csv',
button_label = 'Download dataset from `ecodata`',
button_type = 'default',
has_icon = TRUE,
icon = 'fa fa-save',
class = 'hvr-sweep-to-left',
csv2 = F),
silent = TRUE)
```

## Introduction to Indicator
Diversity metrics may be useful in determining shifts in species composition through time. The shannon-diversity index, a way to measure diversity using species richness and evenness is implemented in the attempt to determine changes over time. The index has a maximum value when all species are equally abundant.

## Key Results and Visualizations
A significant long term decline in the diversity metric is apparent in Georges Bank in both the Spring and the Fall. This is not seen in Gulf of Maine or the Mid Atlantic

**Mid-Atlantic**
```{r plot_survey_shannonMidAtlantic}
# Plot indicator
ggplotObject <- ecodata::plot_survey_shannon(report = 'MidAtlantic', n = 10)
ggplotObject
try(downloadthis::download_this(
ggplotObject$data,
output_name = 'survey_shannonMidAtlantic',
output_extension = '.csv',
button_label = 'Download the data plotted above',
button_type = 'default',
has_icon = TRUE,
icon = 'fa fa-save',
class = 'hvr-sweep-to-left',
csv2 = F),
silent = TRUE)
```
<br>

**New England**
```{r plot_survey_shannonNewEngland}
# Plot indicator
ggplotObject <- ecodata::plot_survey_shannon(report = 'NewEngland', n = 10)
ggplotObject
try(downloadthis::download_this(
ggplotObject$data,
output_name = 'survey_shannonNewEngland',
output_extension = '.csv',
button_label = 'Download the data plotted above',
button_type = 'default',
has_icon = TRUE,
icon = 'fa fa-save',
class = 'hvr-sweep-to-left',
csv2 = F),
silent = TRUE)
```
<br>

## Implications
A decline in the shannon diversity index over time could be a result of several factors; a decline in the number of species being found in the system over time or a select few becoming more abundant (relative to the rest) over time. If the number of species remain contant through time, then the decline represents a shift to a more unbalanced, uneven system where some species are more abundannt that others. An increase would represent the opposite, a shift, in direction, to a more balanced, even system.

If the number of species is changing through time, the inference is more complicated since the maximum value of the shannon-index is a function of the number of species.

## Indicator statistics 
Spatial scale: EPU

Temporal scale: SPRING, FALL

**Variable definitions**

```{r vars_survey_shannon}
# Pull all var names
vars <- ecodata::survey_shannon |>
   dplyr::select(Var, Units) |>
   dplyr::distinct()

DT::datatable(vars)
```
## Get the data

**Point of contact**: [andrew.beet@noaa.gov](mailto:andrew.beet@noaa.gov){.email}

**ecodata dataset**: `ecodata::survey_shannon`

**Tech Doc link**: <https://noaa-edab.github.io/tech-doc/survey_shannon.html>

### Public Availability

Source data are publicly available.
